title: Microsoft Defender for Endpoint API - Auth, Base URI, And Versioning Conventions
applies_to: App-only and delegated access to Defender for Endpoint data

confirmed_facts.fact: Microsoft Defender for Endpoint API access requires OAuth 2.0 authentication through Microsoft Entra ID and supports both application context and user context access.
When granting API permissions in Microsoft Entra ID, the resource is still discovered under the legacy name WindowsDefenderATP.
The current service base URI is https://api.security.microsoft.com and OData-style queries use the /api prefix.
The API supports versioning, and v1.0 can be requested explicitly with URLs such as https://api.security.microsoft.com/api/v1.0/alerts.

For better performance, Microsoft documents regional service hosts such as us.api.security.microsoft.com, eu.api.security.microsoft.com, uk.api.security.microsoft.com, au.api.security.microsoft.com, swa.api.security.microsoft.com, ina.api.security.microsoft.com, and aea.api.security.microsoft.com.

Some Microsoft Defender for Endpoint APIs continue to require access tokens issued for the legacy resource https://api.securitycenter.microsoft.com, and a token with the wrong audience can fail with 403 even when the endpoint host is https://api.security.microsoft.com.
The Hello World flow for application context uses client credentials against the tenant OAuth endpoint and demonstrates requesting the legacy Defender resource audience.

implementation_notes: Prefer application context for unattended automation, including Logic Apps or other scheduled reporting workflows, unless a specific Defender API requires user context.
Check the Permissions section of the exact method you plan to call; Defender for Endpoint permission names differ by entity family such as Machine, Alert, Vulnerability, Recommendation, and Score.
If requests fail with 403 even though the permission grant looks correct, verify the token audience against the API family's documented expectation before debugging scopes further.
Pick a regional endpoint close to the workload when latency or throughput matters, but keep the documented base URI model in the knowledge so callers understand the canonical shape.
caveats: The product name is Microsoft Defender for Endpoint, but some Entra app-registration and token-audience details still use legacy WindowsDefenderATP or securitycenter identifiers.
US Government customers use different URIs.
Permission grants alone are not enough in delegated flows; user roles and device group visibility can also limit data returned.
